stem: By the Cayley–Hamilton theorem, every \( 2 \times 2 \) matrix satisfies \( A^{2} = (\operatorname{tr}A)\,A - (\det A)\,I \). For \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), what is the \( (1,1) \) entry (top-left entry) of \( A^{2} \)?
solution: The \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A^{2} \) is the first row of \( A \) dotted with the first column of \( A \): \( ([[a]])([[a]]) + ([[b]])([[c]]) = [[a]]^{2} + [[b]]\cdot[[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the \( bc \) contribution from the off-diagonal entries.
This uses \( bd \) instead of \( bc \) for the cross term.
This subtracts the cross term instead of adding it.
This squares the wrong entry, using \( c^{2} \) rather than \( bc \).
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is two less than the correct entry.

stem: By the Cayley–Hamilton theorem, \( A^{2} - (\operatorname{tr}A)A + (\det A)I = 0 \), which rearranges to \( A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det A}\left((\operatorname{tr}A)I - A\right) \). For \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), use this formula to find the \( (1,2) \) entry (top-right entry) of \( A^{-1} \).
solution: In \( (\operatorname{tr}A)I - A \), the \( (1,2) \) entry is \( 0 - [[b]] = -[[b]] \). Dividing by \( \det A = [[det]] \) gives \( A^{-1}_{12} = \dfrac{-[[b]]}{[[det]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign; the \( (1,2) \) entry of \( (\operatorname{tr}A)I - A \) is \( -b \), not \( b \).
This uses the \( (1,1) \) entry \( a \) of \( A \) instead of the \( (1,2) \) entry \( b \).
This uses the diagonal entry \( d \) instead of the off-diagonal entry \( b \).
This uses the other off-diagonal entry \( c \) instead of \( b \).
This is one more than the correct entry.
This is one less than the correct entry.

stem: Repeatedly applying \( A^{2} = (\operatorname{tr}A)A - (\det A)I \) lets any power of a \( 2 \times 2 \) matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) be reduced to the form \( A^{3} = pA + qI \) for constants \( p, q \). What is \( p \)?
solution: Since \( A^{3} = (\operatorname{tr}A)A^{2} - (\det A)A = (\operatorname{tr}A)\big[(\operatorname{tr}A)A - (\det A)I\big] - (\det A)A = \big[(\operatorname{tr}A)^{2} - \det A\big]A - (\operatorname{tr}A)(\det A)I \), the coefficient of \( A \) is \( p = (\operatorname{tr}A)^{2} - \det A = ([[a]]+[[d]])^{2} - ([[a]]\cdot[[d]]-[[b]]\cdot[[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( -\det A \) correction, keeping only \( (\operatorname{tr}A)^{2} \).
This is just \( \det A \), not the full reduction coefficient \( (\operatorname{tr}A)^{2}-\det A \).
This adds \( \det A \) instead of subtracting it.
This uses \( \operatorname{tr}A \) itself instead of \( (\operatorname{tr}A)^{2}-\det A \).
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is three less than the correct coefficient.

stem: A \( 2 \times 2 \) matrix \( A \) satisfies the Cayley–Hamilton relation \( A^{2} = [[p]]A + ([[q]])I \). What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: Matching \( A^{2} = [[p]]A + ([[q]])I \) against the Cayley–Hamilton form \( A^{2} = (\operatorname{tr}A)A - (\det A)I \) gives \( \operatorname{tr}A = [[p]] \) and \( -\det A = [[q]] \), so \( \det A = -[[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the minus sign linking \( q \) to \( -\det A \).
This confuses the trace coefficient \( p \) with the determinant.
This gets the sign of the trace coefficient wrong instead of negating \( q \).
This adds the two given constants instead of negating just \( q \).
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is one less than the correct determinant.

stem: For a \( 2 \times 2 \) matrix, Cayley–Hamilton gives the trace identity \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{2}) = (\operatorname{tr}A)^{2} - 2\det A \). For \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), what is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{2}) \)?
distractor_rationales: This omits the \( -2\det A \) correction entirely.
This subtracts \( \det A \) once instead of \( 2\det A \).
This adds \( 2\det A \) instead of subtracting it.
This is just \( \det A \), not \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{2}) \).
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is two less than the correct trace.
